notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - CLI vs Frontend testing: first test agent/backend via CLI, then present through Streamlit frontend.
  - Autogen library / agentic AI: using Autogen to build multi-agent workflows (search agent, summarizer, tool integration).
  - Generator/yield pattern: backend yields messages (generator) used by CLI and Streamlit to stream responses in real time.
  - Streamlit UI components used:
    - st.set_page_config: set page title and icon.
    - text box (user query) and slider (number of papers) as inputs.
    - st.button: trigger runner when clicked and query present.
    - container/placeholder (st.placeholder or container) to display streaming messages.
    - st.spinner: show loading state while backend runs.
    - st.markdown: format returned role/content into readable markdown.
  - Backend architecture: frontend calls a runner that invokes literature_review_backend.py (or autogen_backend.py) which runs agent pipeline and returns roles+content entries.
  - Tool integration in agents: sequence tools (e.g., Google search agent -> RF library search) so agents can call external tools in chain.
  - Dockerized execution: running project inside Docker container; agent can install missing Python packages (e.g., pandas) at runtime and execute code (generate graphs, save images).
  - Summarizer agent: an agent that summarizes papers and returns links and condensed content.
  - Event loop considerations: Streamlit creates/uses its own event loop; asynchronous/generator logic needs to account for Streamlit event loop behavior.
  - Dependencies/keys policy: you can avoid requiring Google/Vertex API keys if your LLM is already handled; many docs unnecessarily demand extra cloud keys.
  - Project structure recommendation: clear backend (literature_review_backend.py/autogen_backend.py) + minimal Streamlit front end (~15–40 lines) for presentation.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - Presentation vs terminal: "If you would have been showing on a terminal that is not the right way" (implies Streamlit UI is the better 'presentation' medium).
  - "All the magic is happening" (describing the backend runner area where core logic executes).
  - "I actually wasted an R one day" (metaphor for time lost troubleshooting env issues).

- Any code, commands, or config shown (verbatim as spoken, including likely typos)
  - python 12th
  - autogen back end
  - pip install r requirements
  - which streamllet
  - Streamlit run 12th and stream.app
  - streaml run 12th and stream.app
  - streamlit run 12th and stream.app
  - do deactivate
  - source Autogen bin activate
  - which streamllet (used to show where streamlit binary comes from)
  - st.button (used in conditional to start runner)
  - set page config (st.set_page_config)
  - st.p spinner (spoken; intended st.spinner)
  - st.place that message / chat placeholder / st.place that message (spoken; placeholder/container used)
  - run literature review back end (invoking backend runner)
  - literature review backend.py / autogen_backend.py (backend file names referenced)
  - "search agent" and "summarizer" (agent names)
  - Docker start/refresh (start docker container to run app inside container)
  - Pandas install triggered at runtime (agent noticed pandas not installed and installed required library)
  - Drag-and-drop dataset (Titanic CSV) into UI and query graph generation
  - "click on anyone and go directly to the paper" (UI provides links)

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Streamlit binary path / virtualenv mismatch: Streamlit may use global installation instead of your project's virtualenv; use which streamlit to confirm and re-activate correct venv (deactivate then source <env>/bin/activate) or restart VS Code.
  - Missing module errors (e.g., "no module named RF") can be caused by installing packages into a different Python environment than the one Streamlit uses.
  - Streamlit + async: Streamlit creates/uses event loops; generator-based streaming and async code can create event loop issues — learn about event loops with Streamlit.
  - Avoid unnecessary cloud API keys: many examples ask for Google API/Vertex keys unnecessarily; if your LLM is handled, those extra keys may not be required.
  - Installing requirements: be explicit to use pip install -r requirements.txt (spoken as "pip install r requirements") to ensure all dependencies are installed in correct environment.
  - Container runtime differences: when using Docker, runtime environment differs from host — ensure dependencies inside container are installed.
  - Debugging time sink: env/path issues can be time-consuming (speaker reports wasting a day re-installing a library ten times).

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - CLI test vs Streamlit frontend
    - CLI: quick developer tests, streaming output on terminal.
    - Streamlit: user-friendly UI, interactive widgets, real-time streaming, better presentation.
  - Streamlit UI elements used (could be a table of element -> purpose):
    - st.set_page_config -> page title & icon
    - text input (textbox) -> user query/topic
    - slider -> number of papers to fetch
    - st.button -> start runner
    - container/placeholder -> stream messages/role content
    - st.spinner -> loading state
    - st.markdown -> formatted output display
  - Agent workflow components:
    - Search agent -> collects candidate papers/links
    - RF library tool -> retrieve full text / docs (speaker suggests RF)
    - Summarizer agent -> summarizes and returns condensed content + links
  - Project flow (high level steps):
    - Implement backend agent pipeline (autogen, tools)
    - Test via CLI (generator yield)
    - Build Streamlit frontend to call backend, show streaming responses
    - Dockerize and run container (optional)
    - Showcase (e.g., drag-and-drop Titanic dataset -> agent runs analysis & produces graph)

  - "In many of the example which you will see on the official documentation it unnecessarily asks you for a Google API key or Google uh vertex key or something which I think kind of is very not very helpful"
  - "The reason we are yielding it here because it's a generator it is returning that back."
  - "Always make sure that you do this which streamllet." (spoken; relates to verifying streamlit binary)
  - "I actually wasted an R one day on this particular issue where I installed that library maybe 10 times and genuinely it was very frustrating"
  - "If you would have been showing on a terminal that is not the right way, right?"
  - "This is how your agent will work and a pretty easy maybe 15 20 no 40 line code of streaml net out of which the first yeah 30 line code because the first 10 15 are nothing as such"
  - "Now what I would want what your homework is is that we saw that how can we integrate tools into our agents. I would like that you have a tool here which searches first and then it goes to uh your uh let's say RF library to search that."
  - "We have used an agent for that and see it is saying that okay it sees pandas library is not installed. So it is installing the required library."
  - "This actually completes our crash course."
